What We Monitor / Canned Motor Pumps

Asset type

Canned motor pump monitoring

Sealed pump-motor units with process-lubricated bearings and no sensor access. The motor is inside the can. Vibration sensors cannot mount. Bearings cannot be inspected. The only measurement point that doesn't break containment is the power cable.

Talk to an engineer See detection coverage

The monitoring challenge

Why canned motor pumps are hard to monitor

Sealless design means no external shaft seal, and no access to vibration sensors, bearing condition, or rotor position. The sealed can separates the motor windings from the process fluid, but also blocks mechanical and thermal inspection. Failures cascade: bearing wear drives eccentricity, which stresses the can, which fails and floods the motor within hours.

0

alternative non-intrusive monitoring options for sealed pump-motor units without embedded sensors

40–60%

of canned motor pump failures originate in the sleeve bearings: the component hardest to monitor without embedded position or temperature sensors

0.2–1.0 mm

can thickness separating process fluid from motor windings, when it fails, the motor fails within hours

How ESA works on canned motor pumps

Monitoring through the power cable

ESA measures electrical current and voltage at the motor control cabinet, upstream of the sealed unit. The electrical signal travels via the power cable unaffected by sealed construction. The can material (Hastelloy, Inconel) is non-magnetic; magnetic flux penetrates it. The shorter drivetrain (no coupling, no gearbox) means cleaner baseline spectra. Hydraulic events couple directly from impeller disturbance to rotor rotation to stator current variation.

Electrical faults

Rotor bar breakage and stator winding degradation produce sidebands at characteristic frequencies. Electromagnetic coupling is unaffected by the can.

Load profiling

Dry running, cavitation, and efficiency loss show as step changes or trend deviations in motor current. Direct torque-to-current pathway makes load anomalies visible.

Eccentricity trending

Rotor slot harmonics are sensitive to air gap changes. Small nominal gaps (0.6–1.0 mm) may amplify eccentricity signals relative to standard motors.

What ESA cannot see

Sleeve bearing specific defects (no characteristic frequencies exist), can material degradation, bearing contamination, or thermal gradients in sealed construction.

Detection coverage

What ESA detects: and what it doesn't

Fault What's happening How ESA detects it Confidence
Broken rotor bars Rotor bar cracking or breakage Sidebands at supply ± 2×slip frequency. Electromagnetic fault, unaffected by can construction. Conditional*
Stator winding degradation Insulation breakdown from thermal stress, chemical attack, or can perforation Phase current imbalance and negative sequence components. Electromagnetic, works regardless of sealed housing. Conditional*
Dry running Loss of process fluid: bearing lubrication and cooling fail simultaneously Step change in motor current within seconds. Load change is large and unambiguous. Conditional*
Rotor eccentricity Bearing wear increases clearance, changing air gap geometry Eccentricity harmonics in rotor slot frequency pattern. Small nominal gap (0.6–1.0 mm) may amplify sensitivity. Conditional*
Cavitation Insufficient NPSH causes vapour collapse on impeller Broadband noise elevation in current spectrum. Direct coupling (no mechanical seal losses) may improve signal transfer. Conditional*
Efficiency degradation Impeller wear, internal recirculation, wear ring clearance increase Power-to-load ratio trending over time. Conditional*
Supply-side anomalies Voltage imbalance, harmonics, power quality issues Direct measurement from power supply. Independent of motor construction. Full

*Conditional note: Proven ESA detection physics. Field validation on canned motor pump installations is in progress. Zero confirmed incidents (CIs) to date, confidence ratings reflect theoretical capability, not field-validated performance.

Detection boundaries

No field-validated detection data. Canned motor pumps have zero entries in the SAM4 Detection Accuracy Report. All confidence ratings are based on ESA physics applied to sealed motor designs. Verified accuracy will be published as the monitored fleet grows.
Sleeve bearings have no characteristic defect frequencies. All canned motor pump bearings are journal/sleeve type. BPFO, BPFI, BSF signatures do not exist. ESA detects eccentricity trends and instability, not specific bearing defects.
Can wear is outside ESA's detection capability. Progressive thinning of the containment can (0.2–1.0 mm Hastelloy or Inconel) does not produce validated electrical signatures. ESA detects the consequences of can failure (winding degradation, current imbalance) but not the precursor.
Reduced sensitivity below 5 kW. Most canned motor pumps are 5–50 kW. Below 5 kW, fault sideband amplitudes fall closer to the noise floor. Detection is limited to gross faults and operating condition anomalies.
Larger effective air gap weakens mechanical signatures. The can increases the effective air gap to 0.5–1.5 mm. Mechanical events on the rotor produce proportionally weaker current modulations than in standard motors.

Based on ESA physics extrapolated from validated detection on standard induction motors. No canned-motor-specific field data. See full detection transparency report →

Monitoring architecture

ESA alongside existing canned motor pump instrumentation

No single technology covers all failure modes on a sealed pump-motor unit. The question is which combination provides the best coverage for your risk profile.

ESA (from MCC)
  • Broken rotor bar detection (only non-intrusive method)
  • Stator winding trending (continuous vs periodic testing)
  • Retrofittable to any canned motor pump, no access to sealed unit required
  • Load profiling and efficiency tracking
  • Eccentricity trending via rotor slot harmonics
Overlap
  • Dry running detection (ESA current step-change + process flow/pressure)
  • Operating condition monitoring (ESA load profiling + process parameters)
  • Eccentricity trending (ESA harmonics + embedded position sensors where fitted)
  • Cavitation signatures (ESA noise floor + hydraulic performance metrics)
Embedded sensors & Process monitoring
  • Precise bearing wear measurement (E-Monitor, MAP position sensors)
  • Can integrity monitoring (barrier fluid conductivity)
  • Winding temperature (embedded RTDs)
  • Process fluid condition (moisture, particle count)
  • Pressure and flow rate

ESA does not replace embedded sensors on safety-critical applications. It provides continuous electrical monitoring that fills gaps: especially on units without E-Monitor or MAP systems, and on failure modes (rotor bars, winding condition) that no embedded sensor detects.

Field validation

Building the evidence base

Canned motor pumps are SAM4's newest asset class. Field validation is underway. No confirmed detection incidents have been published yet for this asset type.

Rotor bars Physics

Why we're confident

ESA detection of rotor bars, winding degradation, and dry running is validated on thousands of standard motors. The physics transfers: these are electromagnetic faults detected through the power cable, unaffected by sealed construction.

Based on proven ESA capability
Validation In progress

What we're validating

Eccentricity trending on sleeve bearings, cavitation detection through the can, and the sensitivity threshold for small (5–50 kW) canned motor pumps. Pilot installations are building the dataset.

Fleet data coming 2026
See detection accuracy across all asset types →

Installation on canned motor pumps

Under 60 minutes. No pump access required.

1

Open the motor control cabinet

ESA sensors install at the MCC: the same panel your electricians already access. No process access needed. No system shutdown.

2

Clip sensor onto motor supply cables

Current and voltage sensors clip directly onto the existing cabling. No wiring changes. No interruption to pump operation.

3

Connect and commission

The gateway connects via cellular (4G/LTE). No dependency on your IT network. Monitoring starts immediately. First diagnostic results within 48 hours.

Full installation guide → Security & network architecture →
